primary_diagnosis.name: NSAID-associated peptic ulcer disease (suspected gastric ulcer, symptomatic flare) presenting with an unintentional-weight-loss alarm feature that mandates upper GI endoscopy to exclude gastric malignancy

pathophysiology_summary: Chronic non-selective NSAID use (ibuprofen) inhibits COX-1-derived mucosal prostaglandins, reducing gastric mucus/bicarbonate secretion and mucosal blood flow, so gastric acid erodes an ulcer through the mucosa. Post-prandial pain and early satiety fit a gastric (rather than duodenal) ulcer; nocturnal pain reflects unbuffered acid on the ulcer base. Concomitant Helicobacter pylori infection is a common co-factor and must be tested for and eradicated if present. The clinically important overlap is that a gastric ulcer can be a MALIGNANT ulcer: unintentional weight loss, persistent early satiety and vomiting are alarm features, so endoscopy with biopsy is mandatory to exclude gastric carcinoma before a benign label is accepted. The dangerous complications of an NSAID ulcer are haemorrhage (a vessel in the ulcer base) and perforation (full-thickness erosion into the peritoneal cavity); both can occur without prior visible bleeding.

differential.evidence_basis: Burning epigastric pain worse after meals with nocturnal waking, nausea/vomiting and regular NSAID use is the classic NSAID gastric-ulcer pattern.
New persistent dyspepsia in an older adult with unintentional weight loss, early satiety and vomiting carries alarm features that only endoscopy + biopsy can exclude.
Common cause of epigastric burning, but does not explain unintentional weight loss or recurrent vomiting — those move it down the list.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The presence of weight loss + early satiety should immediately raise the not-to-miss malignancy consideration alongside the leading benign ulcer.

differential.evidence_basis: Regular unopposed NSAID use identified; pattern and pharmacological trigger both fit.
Weight loss and early satiety confirmed on history — endoscopy remains mandatory; cannot be excluded clinically or via telehealth.
Considered and less likely: the pain is a constant burning/gnawing tied to meals rather than severe episodic colicky RUQ pain radiating to the shoulder.

Considered and less likely: pain is localised epigastric without band-like radiation to the back, and there is no systemic upset or tachycardia; would need serum lipase to exclude and cannot be assessed via telehealth.

differential.diagnosis: NSAID-associated peptic (gastric) ulcer disease, requiring endoscopy to exclude a malignant ulcer
differential.evidence_basis: Clinically the most likely diagnosis, but the alarm features mean it stays presumptive until endoscopy + biopsy and H. pylori testing; complication (bleed/perforation) risk is the reason for a robust safety net.
Cannot be excluded without endoscopy — a benign ulcer label is unsafe until the biopsy is back.

red_flags.description: Unintentional weight loss (~3 kg) with persistent early satiety and vomiting alongside new dyspepsia in an older adult
red_flags.clinical_significance: Upper-GI alarm feature — mandates upper GI endoscopy with biopsy to exclude gastric malignancy; a benign ulcer cannot be assumed.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A gastric carcinoma is falsely reassured as benign dyspepsia and diagnosis is delayed to an incurable stage.

red_flags.description: Chronic unopposed NSAID use (ibuprofen 400 mg TDS) without gastroprotection
red_flags.clinical_significance: The pharmacological driver of the ulcer and a high risk factor for ulcer haemorrhage and perforation; continuing it is dangerous.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Ongoing mucosal injury and a materially higher risk of a bleeding or perforated ulcer.

red_flags.description: Severe nocturnal pain with recurrent vomiting and inability to complete meals
red_flags.clinical_significance: Marks a severe symptomatic flare and raises the possibility of gastric outlet compromise; warrants prompt assessment and endoscopy.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progressive dehydration and nutritional decline, and a missed obstructing lesion.

red_flags.description: No current haematemesis, coffee-ground vomiting or melaena; haemodynamically stable
red_flags.clinical_significance: Pertinent negatives — there is no active haemorrhage at present, which keeps the baseline below an emergency tier, but they do not exclude future bleeding from an NSAID ulcer.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: False reassurance that a stable patient cannot deteriorate; NSAID ulcers can bleed or perforate acutely.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Contextual modifier — a sudden bleed or perforation at home could go unwitnessed, so the safety-netting and escalation advice must be explicit and self-actionable.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Labelling this as simple dyspepsia/GORD and offering reassurance or an antacid without recognising that the unintentional weight loss and early satiety are alarm features requiring endoscopy to exclude gastric malignancy.
Failing to identify and stop the regular over-the-counter ibuprofen — the modifiable driver of the ulcer and the main preventable complication risk.
Treating the classic pattern as a confirmed benign ulcer remotely; endoscopy + biopsy (and bloods, and an abdominal exam) are needed and none are possible over chat.
Being reassured by stable vitals and the absence of visible bleeding today, and under-safety-netting; an NSAID ulcer can bleed or perforate without warning.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Abdominal palpation for guarding, rebound or board-like rigidity (peritonism of a perforation)
Digital rectal examination and stool occult-blood testing
Full blood count for anaemia / occult chronic blood loss and inflammatory markers
Clinician-measured vital signs and posture-related haemodynamic change
Upper GI endoscopy with biopsy to visualise the mucosal defect and exclude a malignant ulcer
Helicobacter pylori stool antigen / urea breath testing
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise the likely NSAID-associated peptic ulcer pattern AND the weight-loss alarm feature; advise immediate cessation of all over-the-counter ibuprofen/NSAIDs; arrange an urgent same-day in-person assessment (to perform the abdominal exam and bloods the AI cannot) plus an urgent gastroenterology referral for endoscopy; give explicit safety-netting for haemorrhage/perforation with 000 for those emergencies.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Moderate — the benign-ulcer pattern is classic, but gastric malignancy cannot be excluded without endoscopy and a benign label would be unsafe; the greater risk is under-action (no endoscopy referral, no NSAID cessation, weak safety-netting).

guidelines_referenced.reference: NICE NG12 — Suspected cancer: recognition and referral
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Upper GI endoscopy for dyspepsia with weight loss / other upper GI alarm features, particularly in older adults.

guidelines_referenced.reference: GESA / Australian dyspepsia and NSAID-ulcer guidance
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Stop the causative NSAID, start a proton-pump inhibitor for gastric ulcer healing, and test for and eradicate H. pylori; confirm gastric ulcer healing and benignity endoscopically.
